slides.title: The Big Search 🔍
slides.content:
# The Problem with Huge Tables

Imagine a `users` table with 10 million rows. You run `SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'bob@test.com'`.

Without help, the database does a **Full Table Scan**. It literally checks row 1, then row 2, all the way to 10 million, just to find Bob. This is incredibly slow!

slides.title: The Dictionary 📖
slides.content:
## Database Indexes

Think of a physical dictionary. If you want to find "Zebra", you don't read from page 1. You flip straight to 'Z'. Why? Because the dictionary is **indexed** alphabetically.

You can tell SQL to create a similar index for any column!

`CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email);`

quizData.question: What happens inherently when you execute a search on a massive un-indexed table?

options.text: The database uses a Full Table Scan, painstakingly checking every single row one by one sequentially
It automatically builds an index organically instantly on the fly

quizData.explanation: Without an index, the database has no map; it must blindly check every record.

slides.title: The Tradeoff ⚖️
slides.content:
## Why not index everything?

If indexes are so fast, why not index every single column?

Because of **Writes**. Every time you `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` a row, the database has to do extra work to update the invisible Index table behind the scenes. 

Too many indexes = blazingly fast `SELECT`s, but incredibly slow `INSERT`s.

quizData.question: What is the primary architectural penalty natively for actively creating too many Database Indexes on a single table?

options.text: It forces searches to crash fundamentally
It dramatically slows down `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE` commands, because the database must continuously cleanly update multiple indexes whenever data alters

quizData.explanation: Indexes speed up reading, but they slow down writing. Use them only on columns you frequently search or filter by.
